advaita.rendering: Among the Vedas I am the Sāma-veda — that which is most melodically refined, whose sound-form most fully approaches the non-dual resonance of Brahman. Among the gods I am Vāsava (Indra), lord of the celestials. Among the eleven sense-organs I am manas (mind), the faculty of saṃkalpa (purposive resolve) and vikalpa (oscillating doubt), for it is manas alone that governs inward and outward perception alike. Among living beings I am cetanā (consciousness-function) — the perennial manifestation of buddhi-vṛtti (the arising of cognitive awareness) within the body-sense complex.
advaita.school_anchor: Śaṅkara reads cetanā strictly as buddhi-vṛtti (the cognitive function of the intellect) perpetually manifested in the kārya-karaṇa-saṃghāta (the body-instrument aggregate) — not a separate soul-substance but Brahman's luminosity appearing within the compound. Manas is ranked highest among the indriyas precisely because saṃkalpa-vikalpa is the hinge on which jñāna turns.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: Among the four Vedas — Ṛg, Yajus, Sāman, Atharvan — I am the Sāma-veda, most exalted. Among the gods I am Indra (Vāsava). The sixth-case relationship here is not nirdhāraṇa (selection from a class) but qualification: the beings named are my body, and I am their inner controller. Among the eleven indriyas I am manas, the pre-eminent indriya. Among sentient beings (chetanāvatām) I am cetanā itself — the consciousness that distinguishes the ensouled from the inert.
viśiṣṭādvaita.school_anchor: Rāmānuja explicitly notes this is not nirdhāraṇa (singling out a superior subset) but the śarīra-śarīrin (body-soul) relation: all named entities are Bhagavān's body and He is their antaryāmin (inner ruler). Cetanā here marks the ātman as the defining characteristic of all living beings, not merely a mental function.

dvaita.rendering: Among the Vedas I am the Sāma-veda, most beloved of Hari. Among the devas I am Vāsava, who acts as Hari's instrument. Among the indriyas I am manas, which directs all sense-engagement toward or away from the Lord. Among prāṇas and bhūtas I am cetanā — the dependent consciousness that Hari sustains in every jīva, ever distinct from Him yet ever contingent upon Him.
dvaita.school_anchor: No Madhva bhāṣya on this śloka is present in the payload. This rendering is extrapolated from Madhva's dvaita framework: jīvas are eternally distinct from Viṣṇu; their cetanā is real but wholly paratantra (dependent), never to be collapsed into Brahman.
dvaita.bhāṣya_flag: ABSENT — Madhva bhāṣya explicitly missing from payload ('Sri Madhvacharya did not comment on this sloka'). Rendering is framework-extrapolation, not bhāṣya-anchored.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: Among the Vedas I am the Sāma-veda — whose gāna-mādhurya (the sweetness of melodic chanting) is itself a vibhūti (divine manifestation) to be worshiped in sacrifice. Among the devas I am Indra, the very lord of the yajña-field. Among the eleven indriyas I am manas — and note well: the genitive here is not nirdhāraṇa but intimacy; manas too is an indriya by the prāya-pada convention, and Kṛṣṇa's presence in it is without obstacle. Among bhūtas I am cetanā — the life-awareness that pulses as His own ānanda manifesting in every form.
śuddhādvaita.school_anchor: Vallabha's bhāṣya dwells on gāna-mādhurya as the vehicle of Bhagavad-vibhūti in the Sāma-veda and flags the grammatical point that this genitive is not nirdhāraṇa. He reads manas-as-indriya as prasāda: even mental faculties are sites of Kṛṣṇa's unobstructed (nirbādham) presence.

bhakti.rendering: Among the Vedas I am the Sāma-veda. Among the gods I am Vāsava (Indra). Among the indriyas I am manas. Among all beings I am cetanā — the jñāna-śakti (power of knowing) that belongs to them, by which they are sentient at all.
bhakti.school_anchor: Śrīdhara Svāmī identifies cetanā as jñāna-śakti (the power of cognition) that is intrinsic to bhūtas as their relational characteristic (bhūtānāṃ saṃbandhini). His gloss is compact and devotionally neutral: cetanā is what makes any being a being capable of receiving grace.
bhakti.bhāṣya_flag: Bhāṣya present and used (concise payload; short rendering reflects the source).

advaita-bhakti.rendering: Among the four Vedas I am the Sāma-veda — made supremely beautiful by gāna-mādhurya (the sweetness of its melodic form), enchanting to Kṛṣṇa himself. Among all devas I am Vāsava-Indra, their sovereign. Among the eleven indriyas I am manas, their pravartaka (mover and activator). Among all bhūtas and their transformations I am cetanā — the buddhi-vṛtti (cognitive arising) that discloses cit (pure consciousness), the brilliance of the Lord shining through the finite mind.
advaita-bhakti.school_anchor: Madhusūdana synthesizes: cetanā is cidabhivyañjikā buddher vṛttiḥ — the function of buddhi that manifests cit-consciousness, preserving Śaṅkara's epistemological precision while reading it as Kṛṣṇa's own luminosity radiating through every cognizing being. Gāna-mādhurya as atirama ṇīya (supremely delightful) carries devotional rapture into what Advaita reads as dry vibhūti.

so_what_questions: If Kṛṣṇa declares himself to be manas among the indriyas, what does that imply about the spiritual status of mental resolve (saṃkalpa) — is the mind itself a site of the sacred, or merely the most powerful obstacle?

The verse pairs the Sāma-veda's melodic excellence with Indra's sovereign power and manas's primacy — what is the structural principle that links these three: is it excellence within a category, or the capacity to integrate and govern?

Cetanā is claimed by Śaṅkara as buddhi-vṛtti (a cognitive function) and by Rāmānuja as the defining mark of sentient beings (chetanāvatām) — what is at stake theologically in this disagreement about what consciousness is?
If cetanā (awareness) is literally Kṛṣṇa, what changes in the way a practitioner relates to their own moments of clarity, attention, or insight?

The Sāma-veda is chosen not for doctrinal content but for aesthetic-sonic excellence (gāna-mādhurya) — does this suggest that beauty itself is a mode of divine self-disclosure, and if so, how does that challenge purely doctrinal approaches to scripture?

Vallabha notes the genitive here is not nirdhāraṇa (selection-from-class) but a sign of intimate presence — what would it mean to read all genitive relationships in life (my friend, my work, my body) as Kṛṣṇa's presence rather than possession?

Madhva's silence on this verse is itself data — what might it indicate about the verses a strictly paratantra (dependence-emphasizing) school finds less tractable, and what does that absence reveal about doctrinal priorities?

everyday_applications.advaita: When you notice the mind (manas) oscillating between resolve and doubt (saṃkalpa-vikalpa) in a difficult decision, recognize that very oscillation as the site where Brahman's luminosity is active. Rather than fighting the mind's movement, watch it as the cit-function in operation — the knower behind the knowing is already free.
everyday_applications.viśiṣṭādvaita: Treat every moment of consciousness — waking up, attending to a task, noticing beauty — as the antaryāmin (inner controller) making himself known through your sentience. Your awareness is not yours alone; it is His body's self-awareness. Offer each cognition back as kainkarya (service).
everyday_applications.dvaita: In any creative or intellectual work, recall that the cetanā (awareness) animating you is wholly sustained by Hari — it is paratantra (dependent), never self-originating. This is not diminishment but liberation from the exhaustion of self-authorship: the intelligence operating through you is His gift, and its fruit belongs to Him.
everyday_applications.śuddhādvaita: When you encounter music, chant, or sound that moves you — the gāna-mādhurya (melodic sweetness) that bypasses argument and reaches the heart — receive it as direct prasāda, Kṛṣṇa's own vibhūti arriving through the Sāma-veda register. Let aesthetic delight be a legitimate form of darśana.
everyday_applications.bhakti: Recognize that your jñāna-śakti (the capacity to know and understand anything at all) is itself Kṛṣṇa. When you grasp a truth — in study, in conversation, in a moment of honest self-seeing — that understanding is not merely yours. Offer the clarity itself in bhakti, as you would offer a flower.
everyday_applications.advaita-bhakti: In contemplative practice, when the buddhi momentarily becomes transparent — when a thought clears and pure awareness (cit) shines through — that is cidabhivyañjikā, consciousness disclosing itself. Do not skip past it. Rest there. This is Kṛṣṇa as cetanā, and Madhusūdana says it is supremely beautiful (atirama ṇīya), worth lingering in.

self_assessment: Six genuinely divergent renderings anchored in supplied bhāṣya prose; Madhva absence flagged honestly; cetanā disagreement (buddhi-vṛtti vs jñāna-śakti vs sentience-marker) surfaces real doctrinal fault-line; so-what questions non-obvious; everyday-applications school-distinct.
